quick_facts.label: Core work
quick_facts.value: Social workers assess client and family situations, provide counselling and social-service assistance, maintain case records and reports, and coordinate support such as child-care, adoption, rehabilitation, medical-care or other welfare referrals. The exact caseload and remit depend on the setting and specialization.

quick_facts.label: MSW route
quick_facts.value: An MSW/MA in Social Work is a principal postgraduate route into professional social-work roles. IGNOU’s MSW accepts graduates meeting its 10+2+3 requirement and includes compulsory, elective and fieldwork components; employers may additionally specify relevant experience or a specialist focus.

quick_facts.label: Practice-based preparation
quick_facts.value: Formal social-work education commonly incorporates supervised fieldwork, placements or internships alongside coursework. IGNOU describes a 72-credit MSW including fieldwork, while TISS social-work curricula allocate substantial credits to block/concurrent fieldwork and research.

quick_facts.label: Employment settings
quick_facts.value: Potential settings include government social-sector roles, NGOs and international development organisations, research, child-care and adoption services, and hospital or public-health services. Individual vacancies determine the title, eligibility and responsibilities.

sections.heading: Responsibilities and scope of practice
paragraphs: A social worker’s work is centred on helping people, families, groups or communities address social, psychological, economic, health or protection-related problems. In the National Career Service profile for child and family social work, this includes interviewing clients to assess circumstances and needs; counselling individuals, families, groups and communities; supporting social adjustment and rehabilitation; and helping arrange services such as adoption, foster care, child care or medical care.

Case management is a material part of the role: practitioners document case histories, prepare reports, consult relevant stakeholders, and connect clients to appropriate support. In medical and public-health settings, vacancy and recruitment materials commonly pair an MSW qualification with experience in welfare or health agencies, illustrating that the work may be integrated with multidisciplinary service delivery.

Responsibilities are setting-specific. For example, a child-and-family worker may work with parents, schools and protection services, whereas a medical social worker may focus on patients and families interacting with hospital, welfare and public-health systems.
bullets: Assess needs through client, family or group interviews.
Provide supportive counselling and problem-solving assistance within the job’s scope.
Maintain case records and prepare reports.
Coordinate with families, schools, health providers, welfare agencies and other stakeholders.
Identify, explain and facilitate access to relevant services, benefits or referrals.

sections.heading: Entering the profession after an MSW
paragraphs: For this node, the immediate educational parent is the MSW (Master of Social Work). A practical route is to complete a recognised bachelor’s degree, enter an MSW/MA Social Work programme, and use fieldwork, placements and research projects to develop practice experience. IGNOU lists graduation under the 10+2+3 pattern as its MSW eligibility and states that the programme includes compulsory courses, electives and fieldwork.

There is no single job advertisement that covers every social-work position in India. Requirements should be checked role by role: the National Career Service child-and-family profile lists an MSW as the minimum qualification, while AIIMS medical-social-service recruitment materials require a recognised master’s degree in social work and may require relevant welfare, hospital, medical or public-health experience.

Applicants can make their preparation more targeted by selecting fieldwork and study relevant to the intended area of practice. Current TISS social-work programmes illustrate possible thematic directions including counselling, community organisation and development practice, children and families, disability studies and action, criminology and justice, and livelihoods/social entrepreneurship.
bullets: Complete an MSW/MA Social Work programme that includes substantive field education.
Treat supervised fieldwork, internships and block placements as evidence-building opportunities for entry-level applications.
Read each recruitment notice closely for degree-recognition, marks, age, experience, specialisation and selection requirements.
For health-sector social-work roles, seek relevant welfare, hospital, medical or public-health exposure where vacancies request it.

sections.heading: Skills to develop
paragraphs: The role requires empathetic, structured engagement with people in difficult circumstances. The National Career Service profile identifies abilities in delivering social services, assisting families, counselling individuals and communities, understanding child-abuse and discipline-related legal issues, and identifying causes of problems in collaboration with relevant stakeholders.

Training outcomes published by IGNOU add transferable professional capabilities: communication with a broad range of people, advocacy and networking for clients, collaboration, resource management, problem solving, critical thinking, adaptability, ethical social-work research and fieldwork, and handling complex organisational or stakeholder issues.

These skills need to be applied with professional boundaries and according to the employer’s policies, referral protocols and legal responsibilities; an MSW does not make every graduate qualified to provide specialised clinical or mental-health services independently.
bullets: Interviewing, listening, rapport-building and needs assessment.
Counselling and communication appropriate to individuals, families, groups and communities.
Accurate case documentation, report writing and record management.
Advocacy, service navigation, networking and interdisciplinary coordination.
Research literacy, critical thinking, problem solving and ethical fieldwork.
Contextual knowledge of welfare systems, safeguarding and applicable legal issues.

sections.heading: Where social workers work
paragraphs: Workplaces span community and social-sector organisations, government services, NGOs, international development organisations, research settings and specialised services. IGNOU lists government social-sector employment, NGOs, INGOs, UN organisations and research among MSW prospects; the National Career Service profile also identifies NGOs, child-care centres and adoption centres as probable employers for child-and-family work.

Hospitals and public-health services are another important setting. AIIMS recruitment material for medical social worker roles calls for a master’s degree in social work and relevant welfare or health-agency experience, with medical or public-health exposure preferred in the cited notice. In such settings, the social worker’s work is linked to the needs of patients and families as well as the service system.

Working patterns, travel, field visits, office hours and shift requirements vary by employer and programme area. The NCS profile notes that schedules can vary and that shift work may be available, so applicants should confirm local conditions during recruitment.
bullets: Government social-sector and welfare services.
NGOs, community organisations and international-development agencies.
Child-care, child-protection and adoption-related services.
Hospitals, medical-social-service departments and public-health projects.
Research, advocacy and programme-focused organisations.

sections.heading: Progression and specialisation
paragraphs: Career development can involve moving into a specialist service area, building responsibility for more complex cases or programmes, taking supervisory or management responsibilities, or progressing into research and higher education. TISS’s current programme portfolio demonstrates the breadth of social-work specialisation available, including children and families, community organisation and development practice, disability studies and action, criminology and justice, and livelihoods/social entrepreneurship.

Formal progression depends on the employer’s cadre rules rather than a single national ladder. As one health-sector example, AIIMS proposed medical-social-service cadre rules describe progression from Medical Social Service Officer Grade II to Grade I after three years of service, then to Supervising Medical Social Service Officer after five years in Grade I, and to Chief Medical Social Service Officer after five years in the supervising grade. This is an institutional example, not a universal pathway for all social workers.

For sustained progression, choose roles and field placements that deepen a coherent area of practice, retain evidence of supervised work and outcomes, and monitor recruitment rules for the sector and organisation of interest.
bullets: Develop a practice focus through fieldwork and early roles.
Build experience in casework, programme implementation, community practice, health, protection, disability, justice or livelihoods, as relevant.
Progression to supervisory or senior roles is employer- and cadre-specific.
Research and doctoral study are possible further directions after an MSW.
